content.problemStatement:
Janmansh and Jay are playing a game. They start with a number XXX and they play a total of YYY moves. Janmansh plays the first move of the game, after which both the players make moves alternatingly.

In one move, a player can increment or decrement XXX by 111.

If the final number after performing YYY moves is even, then Janmansh wins otherwise, Jay wins. Determine the winner of the game if both the players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains two integers XXX, YYY - the starting number and the total number of moves respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output the winning player (`Janmansh` or `Jay`).


You may print each character of `Janmansh` and `Jay` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `JAY`, `jaY`, `JAy` will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y — the starting number and the total number of moves.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the name of the winner: "Janmansh" if the final number is even, otherwise "Jay".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: X=2, Y=2 → X+Y=4 (even) → Janmansh wins.\nTest case 2: X=4, Y=3 → X+Y=7 (odd) → Jay wins.
1) 1+1=2 even → Janmansh.\n2) 5+2=7 odd → Jay.\n3) 10+5=15 odd → Jay.

solution.approach: Each move changes the number by ±1, which flips its parity. After Y moves the parity has been flipped Y times. Therefore the final parity is (X parity) XOR (Y parity). The final number is even iff (X + Y) is even. So Janmansh wins when (X + Y) % 2 == 0, otherwise Jay wins. No deeper strategy is needed because every possible sequence of moves yields the same parity.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
